questions.questionText: In uncontrolled diabetes mellitus, the liver shifts to gluconeogenesis and ketogenesis because:
questions.optionB: Insulin deficiency triggers increased lipolysis → excess acetyl-CoA from β-oxidation overwhelms TCA cycle → diverted to ketone body synthesis; OAA is used for gluconeogenesis
questions.optionC: Glucose is being stored as glycogen excessively
questions.optionD: Glucagon is suppressed
questions.explanation: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) metabolic integration: Insulin deficiency + glucagon excess → (1) Peripheral lipolysis (HSL activated) → massive FFA release → hepatic β-oxidation → excess acetyl-CoA. (2) OAA diverted to gluconeogenesis (PEPCK induced by glucagon) → OAA depleted from TCA cycle → acetyl-CoA CANNOT enter TCA → diverted to ketogenesis (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate, acetone). (3) Hepatic gluconeogenesis increases (substrates: glycerol from lipolysis, amino acids from proteolysis, lactate). Result: hyperglycemia + ketonemia + metabolic acidosis. High NADH/NAD+ ratio favors β-hydroxybutyrate over acetoacetate. Reference: Harper's — DKA Metabolic Integration.

questions.questionText: In the fed state, insulin activates all of the following pathways EXCEPT:
questions.optionC: Gluconeogenesis (insulin SUPPRESSES gluconeogenesis — represses PEPCK and G6Pase)
questions.optionD: Lipogenesis (fatty acid synthase and ACC induction)
questions.explanation: Fed state — insulin dominant: ACTIVATED: glycolysis (glucokinase, PFK-1, pyruvate kinase), glycogenesis (glycogen synthase dephosphorylated = active), lipogenesis (ACC, FAS induced), protein synthesis, pentose phosphate pathway. SUPPRESSED: gluconeogenesis (PEPCK, F-1,6-BPase, G6Pase repressed), glycogenolysis, lipolysis, proteolysis, β-oxidation (malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I), ketogenesis. Fasting state — glucagon dominant: opposite effects. Understanding fed/fasted state metabolic switches is crucial for integrative biochemistry questions. Reference: Harper's — Fed State Metabolism.

questions.questionText: A newborn presents with lethargy, poor feeding, and hyperammonemia. The urine shows elevated orotic acid. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.optionA: Ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency (X-linked, excess carbamoyl phosphate diverted to pyrimidine pathway → orotic aciduria)
questions.optionD: Argininosuccinate lyase deficiency
questions.explanation: OTC deficiency: MOST COMMON urea cycle disorder (X-linked recessive → affected boys, carrier females variable). Without OTC: carbamoyl phosphate (from CPS-I in mitochondria) leaks to cytoplasm → enters pyrimidine synthesis pathway → excess orotate (orotic aciduria). Key distinguishing feature: hyperammonemia WITH orotic aciduria. CPS-I deficiency: hyperammonemia WITHOUT orotic aciduria (no excess carbamoyl phosphate for pyrimidine pathway). Hereditary orotic aciduria (UMP synthase deficiency): orotic aciduria WITHOUT hyperammonemia (pyrimidine synthesis defect, not urea cycle). This differential is extremely high-yield. Reference: Harper's — OTC Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Cori cycle (lactic acid cycle) involves metabolic cooperation between:
questions.optionD: Muscle and liver (lactate from anaerobic glycolysis in muscle → gluconeogenesis in liver → glucose returned to muscle)
questions.explanation: Cori cycle: inter-organ metabolic cycle. During exercise/anaerobic conditions: (1) Muscle: glucose → pyruvate → lactate (glycolysis, LDH), (2) Lactate released to blood, (3) Liver: lactate → pyruvate → glucose (gluconeogenesis — requires 6 ATP per glucose), (4) Glucose returned to blood → muscle. Net cost: 6 ATP in liver per glucose recycled (energy sourced from hepatic fatty acid oxidation). Not energy-efficient but prevents lactic acidosis. Glucose-alanine cycle: similar but muscle transamination of pyruvate → alanine instead of lactate (also delivers nitrogen to liver for urea synthesis). Reference: Harper's — Cori Cycle.

questions.questionText: During prolonged starvation (>3 weeks), the brain adapts to use which alternative fuel?
questions.optionD: Ketone bodies (acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate — can supply up to 75% of brain's energy needs)
questions.explanation: Starvation metabolic adaptation: Early (hours-1 day): hepatic glycogenolysis → glucose. Short-term (1-3 days): gluconeogenesis from amino acids (muscle proteolysis), glycerol. Prolonged (>1 week): lipolysis → FA oxidation → ketogenesis increases dramatically → ketone bodies rise in blood. Brain adaptation: (1) Brain CANNOT use fatty acids (FAs don't cross BBB), (2) Brain gradually increases ketone body utilization (from ~0% to ~60-75% of energy), (3) This SPARES muscle protein (reduces proteolysis for gluconeogenesis — survival advantage). Heart, kidney always use ketone bodies efficiently. Reference: Harper's — Starvation Adaptation.

questions.questionText: Which metabolite connects glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, TCA cycle, fatty acid synthesis, and amino acid metabolism?
questions.optionD: Acetyl-CoA (central metabolic hub at the intersection of multiple pathways)
questions.explanation: Acetyl-CoA: the most important metabolic intermediate/hub. Sources: pyruvate (PDH), fatty acid β-oxidation, ketogenic amino acids, ethanol (via acetaldehyde). Fates: (1) TCA cycle (oxidation for energy), (2) Fatty acid synthesis (lipogenesis — via citrate shuttle), (3) Cholesterol synthesis (via HMG-CoA → mevalonate), (4) Ketone body synthesis (ketogenesis — liver mitochondria), (5) Acetylation reactions (histone acetylation, drug metabolism). Acetyl-CoA CANNOT be converted to glucose (irreversible PDH reaction, no net gluconeogenesis from acetyl-CoA in animals). Pyruvate is also crucial (connects glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, TCA, amino acid metabolism). Reference: Harper's — Acetyl-CoA Hub.

questions.questionText: Von Gierke disease (GSD type I) leads to hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, lactic acidosis, and hyperuricemia. The hyperuricemia occurs due to:
questions.optionD: Increased degradation of purine nucleotides (G6P accumulation → increased HMP shunt → increased ribose-5-P → increased PRPP → increased de novo purine synthesis → increased uric acid)
questions.explanation: Von Gierke disease hyperuricemia — integrated mechanism: G6Pase deficiency → G6P trapped inside cells → (1) G6P shunted through HMP pathway → increased ribose-5-P → increased PRPP → stimulates de novo purine synthesis → increased purine degradation → hyperuricemia, (2) Lactic acidosis (increased glycolysis) → lactic acid competes with uric acid for renal excretion → decreased uric acid excretion. Both mechanisms contribute: increased production + decreased excretion = severe hyperuricemia → gouty arthritis. This is an excellent example of how a single enzyme deficiency causes multi-system metabolic derangements. Reference: Harper's — GSD I Metabolic Effects.

questions.questionText: Alcohol metabolism in the liver causes increased NADH/NAD+ ratio leading to all EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: Enhanced gluconeogenesis (gluconeogenesis is INHIBITED by high NADH — OAA → malate shift depletes gluconeogenic substrates)
questions.optionC: Fatty liver (increased fatty acid synthesis, decreased β-oxidation)
questions.optionD: Hypoglycemia
questions.explanation: Alcohol metabolism → NADH/NAD+ ratio increased: Ethanol → acetaldehyde (alcohol dehydrogenase, NAD+→NADH) → acetate (aldehyde dehydrogenase, NAD+→NADH). Consequences of high NADH: (1) Pyruvate → lactate (LDH) → lactic acidosis AND depleted pyruvate for gluconeogenesis. (2) OAA → malate (MDH) → depleted OAA for gluconeogenesis AND TCA cycle. (3) DHAP → glycerol-3-P → TAG synthesis → fatty liver. (4) Impaired β-oxidation (NADH inhibits). (5) Hypoglycemia (gluconeogenesis inhibited). Gluconeogenesis is INHIBITED, not enhanced. Reference: Harper's — Alcohol and Metabolic Effects.

questions.questionText: Pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) deficiency results in lactic acidosis because:
questions.optionC: Pyruvate cannot be converted to acetyl-CoA → pyruvate accumulates → converted to lactate by LDH; also impairs TCA cycle entry
questions.optionD: Gluconeogenesis is blocked
questions.explanation: PDH deficiency: X-linked dominant (PDH E1α subunit). PDH complex: pyruvate + CoA + NAD+ → acetyl-CoA + CO2 + NADH. Without PDH: pyruvate accumulates → equilibrium shift → lactate (LDH) and alanine (ALT) increase → lactic acidosis. Acetyl-CoA not generated → TCA cycle starved → energy deficit → neurological damage. Brain and nervous system most affected (glucose-dependent). Features: lactic acidosis, developmental delay, seizures, microcephaly. Treatment: ketogenic diet (provides acetyl-CoA directly from fatty acid oxidation, bypassing PDH), thiamine supplementation (some thiamine-responsive forms). Reference: Harper's — PDH Deficiency.

questions.questionText: The biochemical basis of why fats cannot be converted to glucose in animals is:
questions.optionA: Fatty acids are too large to enter hepatocytes
questions.optionB: Acetyl-CoA (from β-oxidation) cannot yield net glucose because PDH is irreversible and 2 carbons are lost as CO2 in TCA per acetyl-CoA entering
questions.optionD: NADPH is insufficient
questions.explanation: Fat → glucose conversion impossible (net) in animals: Even-chain fatty acids → acetyl-CoA (β-oxidation). Acetyl-CoA enters TCA cycle: 2 carbons enter as acetyl-CoA, 2 carbons leave as CO2 (at isocitrate dehydrogenase and α-KGDH steps) → NO NET carbon gain for gluconeogenesis. PDH reaction (pyruvate → acetyl-CoA) is IRREVERSIBLE → cannot reverse to make pyruvate → cannot make glucose. Exception: ODD-chain FAs → propionyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA → OAA → glucose (glucogenic; minor contribution). Glycerol backbone of TAG CAN yield glucose (glycerol → glycerol-3-P → DHAP → gluconeogenesis). Reference: Harper's — Fat Cannot Yield Glucose.
questions.conceptTag: Fats cannot make glucose irreversible PDH

questions.questionText: Which organ-specific biochemical feature distinguishes the liver from muscle in glycogen metabolism?
questions.optionD: Liver has glucose-6-phosphatase (can release free glucose to blood); muscle lacks G6Pase (uses glycogen-derived G6P internally for glycolysis)
questions.explanation: Liver vs muscle glycogen metabolism — key integration: Liver: has GLUCOSE-6-PHOSPHATASE → converts G6P → free glucose → exported to blood to maintain blood glucose (liver glycogen serves the whole body). Responds to glucagon. Muscle: LACKS G6Pase → G6P trapped in muscle cells → enters glycolysis for LOCAL energy production (muscle glycogen serves only muscle). Responds to epinephrine (not glucagon — muscle lacks glucagon receptors). This explains why: McArdle disease (muscle phosphorylase deficiency) → exercise intolerance but normal blood glucose. Von Gierke (liver G6Pase deficiency) → severe fasting hypoglycemia. Reference: Harper's — Organ-Specific Glycogen Metabolism.

questions.questionText: A child with mousy body odor, fair skin, eczema, and developmental delay has elevated phenylalanine. The FULL metabolic consequences of PKU beyond phenylalanine accumulation include:
questions.optionA: Excess Phe → (1) inhibits tyrosinase → decreased melanin (fair skin/hair), (2) inhibits tryptophan hydroxylase → decreased serotonin, (3) inhibits tyrosine hydroxylase → decreased catecholamines (dopamine, NE), (4) transamination to phenylpyruvate/phenylacetate → mousy odor
questions.optionD: Phenylalanine is converted to a toxic metabolite that damages neurons directly
questions.explanation: PKU — comprehensive metabolic derangement: PAH deficiency → Phe accumulates (>20 mg/dL, normal <2). Downstream effects: (1) DECREASED MELANIN: excess Phe competitively inhibits TYROSINASE (both use aromatic AA binding site) → decreased Tyr→DOPA→melanin → fair skin, hair, blue eyes. (2) DECREASED SEROTONIN: Phe competes with Trp for LAT1 (large neutral amino acid transporter) across BBB → decreased brain Trp → decreased serotonin (5-HT). (3) DECREASED CATECHOLAMINES: Phe inhibits tyrosine hydroxylase + competes for brain Tyr entry → decreased dopamine, NE, epinephrine. (4) TRANSAMINATION products: phenylpyruvate, phenyllactate, phenylacetate (mousy/musty odor in urine). (5) MYELIN disruption: unknown exact mechanism but Phe/metabolites interfere with myelination during critical period. Treatment: Phe-restricted diet + Tyr supplementation. BH4 deficiency (malignant PKU): different treatment (BH4, L-DOPA, 5-HTP). Reference: Harper's — PKU Integration.
questions.conceptTag: PKU comprehensive metabolic integration melanin serotonin

questions.questionText: The pentose phosphate pathway provides NADPH for all of the following EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: Oxidative phosphorylation (ETC uses NADH and FADH2, NOT NADPH)
questions.optionD: Cytochrome P450 drug metabolism
questions.explanation: NADPH functions (reductive biosynthesis and antioxidant): (1) Fatty acid synthesis (fatty acid synthase reductive steps), (2) Cholesterol synthesis (HMG-CoA reductase), (3) Steroid hormone synthesis (hydroxylases), (4) Glutathione reduction (GSH recycled from GSSG by glutathione reductase using NADPH), (5) ROS detoxification (protects RBCs from oxidative damage — G6PD deficiency → hemolytic anemia), (6) CYP450 drug metabolism, (7) Respiratory burst (NADPH oxidase in phagocytes — superoxide generation). NADH (not NADPH) feeds into ETC via Complex I for ATP generation. NADPH and NADH serve fundamentally different metabolic roles. Reference: Harper's — NADPH Functions.

questions.questionText: G6PD deficiency causes hemolytic anemia upon oxidative stress because:
questions.optionB: Reduced NADPH generation → impaired glutathione recycling → RBCs cannot neutralize reactive oxygen species → Heinz body formation → hemolysis
questions.optionD: Hemoglobin is structurally abnormal
questions.explanation: G6PD deficiency (X-linked): most common enzymopathy worldwide. G6PD is the ONLY source of NADPH in RBCs (no mitochondria → no other NADPH-generating enzymes). Without NADPH: glutathione reductase cannot regenerate GSH from GSSG → oxidative damage to hemoglobin → Heinz bodies (denatured hemoglobin precipitates) → recognized and removed by splenic macrophages ('bite cells') → extravascular + intravascular hemolysis. Triggers: antimalarials (primaquine), sulfonamides, fava beans (favism), infections, DKA. Diagnosis: G6PD enzyme assay (FALSE NEGATIVE during acute crisis — young RBCs have normal G6PD). Peripheral smear: bite cells, Heinz bodies (supravital stain). Reference: Harper's — G6PD Deficiency.

questions.questionText: Citrate shuttle connects which two metabolic processes?
questions.optionC: Mitochondrial acetyl-CoA production and cytoplasmic fatty acid synthesis (exports acetyl-CoA from mitochondria as citrate)
questions.optionD: Muscle and liver
questions.explanation: Citrate shuttle (tricarboxylate transport): Mitochondrial acetyl-CoA cannot cross inner mitochondrial membrane. Solution: acetyl-CoA + OAA → citrate (citrate synthase, in mitochondria) → citrate exported to cytoplasm → cleaved by ATP-citrate lyase → acetyl-CoA (for FA synthesis) + OAA. Cytoplasmic OAA → malate (MDH, NADH→NAD+) → pyruvate (malic enzyme, NADP+→NADPH — additional NADPH for FA synthesis) → re-enters mitochondria. Net result: transfers acetyl-CoA from mitochondria to cytosol AND generates NADPH. Active in FED state (insulin-stimulated lipogenesis). Reference: Harper's — Citrate Shuttle.

questions.questionText: Reye syndrome in children is characterized by:
questions.optionD: Acute encephalopathy and fatty liver (mitochondrial dysfunction — associated with aspirin use during viral illness, especially varicella/influenza)
questions.explanation: Reye syndrome: acute, potentially fatal condition in children. Pathogenesis: aspirin (salicylate) during viral illness (varicella, influenza B) → mitochondrial damage in liver and brain → impaired β-oxidation, urea cycle, oxidative phosphorylation. Biochemical features: hyperammonemia (urea cycle impaired), hypoglycemia (gluconeogenesis impaired), elevated transaminases, microvesicular fatty infiltration of liver, cerebral edema. Clinical: vomiting → encephalopathy → coma. Reason aspirin is CONTRAINDICATED in children with viral illness (use acetaminophen/ibuprofen instead). Reference: Harper's — Reye Syndrome Biochemistry.

questions.questionText: The malate-aspartate shuttle is important for:
questions.optionA: Transferring cytoplasmic NADH reducing equivalents into mitochondria (since inner membrane is impermeable to NADH)
questions.optionD: Amino acid transport into ER
questions.explanation: Malate-aspartate shuttle: transfers NADH from cytoplasm to mitochondria. Cytoplasmic NADH + OAA → malate (cytoplasmic MDH) → malate enters mitochondria → regenerates NADH + OAA (mitochondrial MDH) → mitochondrial NADH enters Complex I → 2.5 ATP per NADH. OAA cannot cross membrane → converted to aspartate (transamination) → exits mitochondria → reconverted to OAA in cytoplasm. Found in: liver, heart, kidney (tissues with high metabolic activity). Glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle (brain, muscle): FADH2 yield → only 1.5 ATP per cytoplasmic NADH (less efficient). Reference: Harper's — Malate-Aspartate Shuttle.

questions.questionText: A patient on isoniazid (INH) therapy develops peripheral neuropathy. This is due to deficiency of:
questions.optionA: Vitamin B6/pyridoxine (INH inhibits pyridoxine metabolism — PLP is essential for many reactions)
questions.optionD: Folate
questions.explanation: INH-induced B6 deficiency — clinical biochemistry integration: INH reacts with PLP (pyridoxal phosphate, active form of B6) → inactivates it → functional B6 deficiency. PLP is cofactor for: aminotransferases (ALT, AST), decarboxylases (GABA synthesis/GAD, serotonin synthesis, histamine synthesis), ALA synthase (heme synthesis), serine hydroxymethyltransferase, cystathionine β-synthase. B6 deficiency → peripheral neuropathy (impaired nerve function), sideroblastic anemia (impaired heme synthesis → iron-laden mitochondria in RBC precursors), seizures (decreased GABA synthesis). Prevention: pyridoxine supplementation with INH therapy. Also seen with cycloserine, penicillamine. Reference: Harper's — INH and B6.

questions.questionText: The glucose-alanine cycle differs from the Cori cycle in that the glucose-alanine cycle:
questions.optionA: Transports nitrogen from muscle to liver (via alanine) in addition to recycling glucose → alanine used for gluconeogenesis AND provides NH4+ for urea synthesis
questions.optionD: Does not involve the liver
questions.explanation: Glucose-alanine cycle vs Cori cycle: Both recycle carbon skeletons between muscle and liver. Cori cycle: muscle glucose → pyruvate → LACTATE → blood → liver lactate → pyruvate → glucose (transports only carbon). Glucose-alanine cycle: muscle pyruvate + amino group (from BCAA catabolism in muscle, via ALT) → ALANINE → blood → liver alanine → pyruvate (ALT, releases NH4+ → urea cycle) → glucose. Dual purpose: (1) carbon recycling (glucose), (2) nitrogen transport (non-toxic way to carry NH4+ from muscle to liver for urea synthesis). Active during fasting and exercise. Reference: Harper's — Glucose-Alanine Cycle.

questions.questionText: Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome in chronic alcoholics is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionD: Thiamine (vitamin B1 — alcohol impairs thiamine absorption, storage, and activation)
questions.explanation: Wernicke-Korsakoff — thiamine deficiency integration: Alcohol causes B1 deficiency via: decreased dietary intake, impaired intestinal absorption, impaired hepatic storage/phosphorylation, increased urinary excretion. Wernicke encephalopathy (acute): confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, lateral rectus palsy) — MEDICAL EMERGENCY, treat with IV thiamine BEFORE glucose (glucose loading without B1 can precipitate/worsen Wernicke's). Korsakoff psychosis (chronic/irreversible): anterograde amnesia, confabulation, personality changes (mammillary body damage). TPP enzymes affected: PDH, α-KGDH, transketolase (most sensitive — used for diagnostic testing). Reference: Harper's — Wernicke-Korsakoff.

questions.questionText: Lactic acidosis in sepsis/shock occurs due to:
questions.optionC: Tissue hypoperfusion → anaerobic glycolysis (increased pyruvate → lactate conversion) + impaired hepatic lactate clearance
questions.optionD: Renal failure alone
questions.explanation: Lactic acidosis in sepsis — biochemical integration: Type A (most common — tissue hypoxia): inadequate O2 delivery → ETC cannot accept electrons → NADH accumulates → NAD+ depleted → TCA and oxidative phosphorylation impaired → cells forced to use anaerobic glycolysis → pyruvate reduced to lactate (LDH, regenerates NAD+ for continued glycolysis). Simultaneously: liver hypoperfusion → impaired gluconeogenesis from lactate (Cori cycle disrupted). Result: massive lactate accumulation → metabolic acidosis. Type B: mitochondrial dysfunction without hypoxia (drugs like metformin, liver failure, inborn errors). Serum lactate >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion. Reference: Harper's — Lactic Acidosis Biochemistry.

questions.questionText: Zellweger syndrome demonstrates the importance of peroxisomes because deficiency leads to:
questions.optionC: Accumulation of VLCFA, phytanic acid, pipecolic acid, and deficiency of plasmalogens (all peroxisomal functions lost)
questions.optionD: Isolated fatty liver
questions.explanation: Zellweger syndrome (cerebrohepatorenal syndrome): peroxisome biogenesis disorder (PEX gene mutations) → no functional peroxisomes. Lost functions: (1) VLCFA β-oxidation → VLCFA accumulate, (2) α-oxidation (phytanic acid) → phytanic acid accumulates, (3) Plasmalogen synthesis (initial steps peroxisomal) → plasmalogen deficiency, (4) Bile acid synthesis (some steps), (5) Pipecolic acid metabolism → pipecolic acid accumulates, (6) H2O2 metabolism (catalase). Features: characteristic craniofacial dysmorphism, severe hypotonia, seizures, hepatomegaly, renal cysts, epiphyseal stippling, death in first year. Demonstrates peroxisome importance in multiple metabolic pathways. Reference: Harper's — Zellweger Syndrome.

questions.questionText: AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase) acts as a cellular energy sensor and is activated when:
questions.optionA: AMP/ATP ratio increases (low energy state — exercise, fasting, hypoxia) → activates catabolic pathways, inhibits anabolic pathways
questions.optionD: NADPH is abundant
questions.explanation: AMPK: master metabolic regulator — the 'fuel gauge' of the cell. Activated by: high AMP/ATP ratio (energy depletion), exercise, fasting, metformin, thiazolidinediones, adiponectin. AMPK activates catabolic pathways: fatty acid β-oxidation (phosphorylates/inactivates ACC → reduces malonyl-CoA → CPT-I activated), glucose uptake (GLUT4 translocation), autophagy, mitochondrial biogenesis. AMPK inhibits anabolic pathways: fatty acid synthesis (ACC inactivation), cholesterol synthesis (phosphorylates/inactivates HMG-CoA reductase), protein synthesis (inhibits mTOR), glycogen synthesis. Metformin antidiabetic mechanism: activates AMPK. Reference: Harper's — AMPK Energy Sensing.

questions.questionText: Fluoroacetate poisoning is lethal because it:
questions.optionC: Is converted to fluorocitrate which irreversibly inhibits aconitase in the TCA cycle → blocking cellular energy production
questions.optionD: Inhibits glycolysis
questions.explanation: Fluoroacetate: 'lethal synthesis' — fluoroacetate is activated by CoA to fluoroacetyl-CoA → condenses with OAA (citrate synthase) → fluorocitrate. Fluorocitrate is a potent inhibitor of aconitase (converts citrate → isocitrate in TCA cycle) → TCA cycle blocked → citrate accumulates → energy production (ATP) ceases → cell death. Heart (highly aerobic) is most affected → cardiac failure. This demonstrates: (1) critical importance of TCA cycle, (2) concept of lethal synthesis (organism's own enzymes create the toxic metabolite), (3) aconitase as a vulnerable target. Used as rodenticide (compound 1080). Reference: Harper's — Fluoroacetate Poisoning.

questions.questionText: In the fasting state, glucagon activates hepatic gluconeogenesis by inducing:
questions.optionA: PEPCK (phosphoenolpyruvate carboxykinase) and fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase — key gluconeogenic enzymes
questions.optionD: Glucokinase
questions.explanation: Glucagon → cAMP → PKA activation: Gluconeogenic enzymes INDUCED: PEPCK (OAA → PEP, rate-limiting), fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase (F-1,6-BP → F6P), glucose-6-phosphatase (G6P → glucose). Glycolytic enzymes SUPPRESSED: glucokinase (repressed), PFK-1 (inactivated — F-2,6-BP decreased by PKA phosphorylation of bifunctional enzyme → PFK-2 inhibited, F-2,6-BPase activated), pyruvate kinase (phosphorylated = inactive). This reciprocal regulation ensures glycolysis and gluconeogenesis don't occur simultaneously (futile cycle prevention). F-2,6-BP is the most potent allosteric activator of PFK-1 and inhibitor of F-1,6-BPase. Reference: Harper's — Glucagon and Gluconeogenesis.

questions.questionText: A child with edematous malnutrition (kwashiorkor) has a fatty liver but a child with marasmus does not. The biochemical explanation is:
questions.optionC: In kwashiorkor (protein deficiency with relatively adequate calories): liver CANNOT synthesize apolipoproteins (especially ApoB-100) needed for VLDL assembly and secretion → TG accumulates in hepatocytes → fatty liver; in marasmus (total calorie deficiency): fat stores are mobilized and utilized, no excess TG for hepatic accumulation
questions.optionD: Fat metabolism is the same in both
questions.explanation: Kwashiorkor vs marasmus — hepatic fat: KWASHIORKOR (protein-energy malnutrition, predominantly PROTEIN deficient): (1) Carbohydrate intake relatively maintained → insulin secretion continues → lipogenesis maintains TG production in liver. (2) Severe PROTEIN deficiency → cannot synthesize ApoB-100 (lipoprotein structural protein) → VLDL assembly IMPAIRED → TG TRAPPED in liver → FATTY LIVER (hepatomegaly). Also: decreased albumin → edema, decreased acute phase proteins → infection susceptibility. MARASMUS (total calorie deficiency): (1) Minimal calorie intake → low insulin → lipolysis predominates → FFAs mobilized and oxidized for energy, (2) No excess TG delivery to liver, (3) Protein synthesis relatively preserved early → apolipoprotein production maintained → no fatty liver. Edema: kwashiorkor (hypoalbuminemia), absent in marasmus. Reference: Harper's — Kwashiorkor Fatty Liver.
questions.conceptTag: Kwashiorkor fatty liver ApoB VLDL vs marasmus

questions.questionText: Cyanide poisoning causes tissue hypoxia by inhibiting:
questions.optionB: Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) — prevents electron transfer to O2, paralyzing oxidative phosphorylation
questions.optionD: Succinate dehydrogenase
questions.explanation: Cyanide: binds to Fe3+ in cytochrome a/a3 (Complex IV, cytochrome c oxidase) → blocks final electron transfer to O2 → entire ETC backs up → NADH/FADH2 cannot be reoxidized → TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation cease → total dependence on anaerobic glycolysis → lactic acidosis. Cells cannot use O2 even though it's available (histotoxic/cytotoxic hypoxia) → cherry-red venous blood (venous O2 remains high). Treatment: hydroxocobalamin (binds CN- → cyanocobalamin), or nitrites (methemoglobin formation → binds CN-) + thiosulfate (rhodanese converts CN- → thiocyanate, excreted). CO inhibits same complex but binds to Fe2+. Reference: Harper's — Cyanide and ETC.

questions.questionText: Metformin's antidiabetic mechanism involves:
questions.optionA: AMPK activation → decreased hepatic gluconeogenesis and increased peripheral glucose uptake + mild Complex I inhibition
questions.optionB: Increased insulin secretion from β cells
questions.optionD: PPAR-γ agonism
questions.explanation: Metformin — biochemical mechanism: (1) Mildly inhibits mitochondrial Complex I → increases AMP/ATP ratio → activates AMPK, (2) AMPK activation: inhibits gluconeogenesis (represses PEPCK, G6Pase), inhibits lipogenesis (ACC inactivation), enhances glucose uptake (GLUT4), increases fatty acid oxidation. (3) Also reduces intestinal glucose absorption. Does NOT increase insulin secretion (no hypoglycemia risk as monotherapy). No weight gain (may cause modest weight loss). Most prescribed antidiabetic drug worldwide. Contraindicated in: eGFR <30 (lactic acidosis risk due to Complex I inhibition → anaerobic metabolism). Reference: Harper's — Metformin Mechanism.

questions.questionText: Arsenic poisoning interferes with cellular metabolism by:
questions.optionD: Binding to lipoic acid in pyruvate dehydrogenase and α-KGDH (inhibiting their function) and uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation by substituting arsenate for phosphate
questions.explanation: Arsenic dual mechanism: (1) Arsenite (As3+): binds sulfhydryl groups (SH) of lipoic acid → inhibits multi-enzyme complexes requiring lipoic acid: PDH (pyruvate → acetyl-CoA), α-KGDH (TCA cycle), BCKDH (branched-chain AA metabolism). Result: impaired aerobic metabolism → lactate accumulates. (2) Arsenate (As5+): structural analog of phosphate → substitutes for Pi in glycolysis (arsenolysis at GAPDH step → 1-arseno-3-phosphoglycerate formed → spontaneously hydrolyzes → bypasses substrate-level phosphorylation → 'futile' glycolysis, no ATP generated at that step). Combined: cellular energy crisis. Reference: Harper's — Arsenic Toxicity.

questions.questionText: During prolonged starvation (>3 weeks), the brain's primary fuel shifts from glucose to:
questions.optionA: Ketone bodies (β-hydroxybutyrate and acetoacetate) — reducing brain's glucose requirement from ~120g/day to ~40g/day, thereby sparing muscle protein
questions.optionD: Lactate only
questions.explanation: Brain fuel adaptation in starvation: Early (hours-days): brain uses glucose exclusively (fatty acids cannot cross BBB). Days 3-5: liver produces ketone bodies from fatty acid β-oxidation (acetyl-CoA → acetoacetate + β-hydroxybutyrate). After 2-3 weeks: brain derives ~60-70% of energy from ketones → glucose requirement drops from ~120g to ~40g/day. Critical consequence: MUSCLE PROTEIN SPARED (less gluconeogenesis from amino acids needed → urea excretion drops from ~12g to ~3g/day). Without this adaptation, muscle wasting would be lethal within ~10 days. Remaining glucose: from gluconeogenesis (glycerol from lipolysis, alanine from muscle, lactate — Cori cycle). Reference: Harper's — Starvation Adaptation.

questions.questionText: Metabolic syndrome is characterized by insulin resistance. At the biochemical level, elevated free fatty acids (FFAs) in obesity cause insulin resistance by:
questions.optionC: Activating serine/threonine kinases (PKC, JNK, IKKβ) that phosphorylate IRS-1 on SERINE residues (instead of tyrosine) → impaired insulin signaling cascade
questions.optionD: Destroying insulin receptors
questions.explanation: FFA-induced insulin resistance — molecular mechanism: Elevated FFAs (from adipose lipolysis in obesity) → enter cells → intracellular lipid metabolites accumulate (diacylglycerol/DAG, ceramides). DAG activates PKCθ (muscle), PKCε (liver) → activates serine/threonine kinases (JNK, IKKβ). These phosphorylate IRS-1 on SERINE residues (normally, insulin receptor phosphorylates IRS-1 on TYROSINE). Serine-phosphorylated IRS-1 → CANNOT propagate PI3K/AKT pathway → impaired GLUT4 translocation → reduced glucose uptake. Also: chronic inflammation (TNF-α, IL-6 from adipose tissue) activates same kinases. Ceramides: inhibit AKT directly. This creates a vicious cycle: insulin resistance → hyperinsulinemia → more lipogenesis → more FFAs → worse resistance. Basis for thiazolidinedione action (PPARγ agonist). Reference: Harper's — Insulin Resistance.

questions.questionText: Ethanol metabolism produces excess NADH (high NADH/NAD+ ratio). Which of the following metabolic derangements is NOT a consequence?
questions.optionC: Increased gluconeogenesis from amino acids → hyperglycemia
questions.optionD: Fatty liver (impaired fatty acid oxidation due to high NADH)
questions.explanation: Ethanol and NADH excess: Ethanol → acetaldehyde (alcohol dehydrogenase, cytoplasmic NADH generated) → acetate (ALDH, mitochondrial NADH generated). High NADH/NAD+ ratio consequences: (1) HYPOGLYCEMIA: NAD+ is required for gluconeogenesis (OAA → malate reversal blocked, lactate → pyruvate blocked) → gluconeogenesis IMPAIRED (not increased). (2) LACTIC ACIDOSIS: excess NADH drives pyruvate → lactate (LDH). (3) FATTY LIVER: β-oxidation requires NAD+ → inhibited. Excess NADH promotes fatty acid synthesis (via shuttle). Also: acetyl-CoA from ethanol + impaired β-oxidation → TG accumulation. (4) Hyperlipidemia, hyperuricemia (lactate competes with urate for renal excretion). Option C is WRONG — ethanol causes HYPOglycemia, not hyperglycemia. Reference: Harper's — Ethanol Metabolism.

questions.questionText: The liver is unique in its role as 'metabolic hub.' During the fed state, the liver performs all EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: Glycogen synthesis and lipogenesis from excess glucose
questions.optionD: Ketogenesis for peripheral tissue fuel
questions.explanation: Liver in fed vs fasted state: FED state (insulin high, glucagon low): Glycogen synthesis (glucose → G6P → G1P → UDP-glucose → glycogen), Lipogenesis (excess glucose → acetyl-CoA → fatty acids → TG → VLDL secretion), Protein synthesis (albumin, clotting factors, acute phase proteins), Amino acid catabolism → urea cycle, Cholesterol synthesis. FASTED state: Glycogenolysis (first 4-6 hours), Gluconeogenesis (after glycogen depleted), KETOGENESIS (acetyl-CoA from β-oxidation → acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate — exported for brain, muscle), β-oxidation of fatty acids. Ketogenesis occurs ONLY in fasted/starved state (insulin low) — NOT in fed state. The liver makes ketones but CANNOT use them (lacks succinyl-CoA:acetoacetate transferase/thiophorase). Reference: Harper's — Organ-Specific Metabolism.
questions.conceptTag: Liver fed vs fasted metabolism hub

questions.questionText: A marathon runner after 2 hours of exercise has elevated plasma free fatty acids, low insulin, and moderate ketone levels. The primary fuel source for skeletal muscle at this stage is:
questions.optionD: Free fatty acids (from adipose lipolysis activated by low insulin + catecholamines) with muscle glycogen being depleted
questions.explanation: Exercise fuel hierarchy — duration dependent: First 5-10 minutes: muscle creatine phosphate (CP → ATP via creatine kinase), then anaerobic glycolysis (muscle glycogen). 10-40 minutes: aerobic glucose oxidation (muscle glycogen → blood glucose). >40 minutes-2 hours: increasing shift to FREE FATTY ACIDS (FFAs) as primary fuel. Low insulin + high catecholamines (epinephrine) → hormone-sensitive lipase activation in adipose → lipolysis → FFAs released → β-oxidation in muscle. Muscle glycogen progressively depleted ('hitting the wall' in marathons). Moderate ketone elevation: liver producing from excess acetyl-CoA. BCAAs (leucine, isoleucine, valine): oxidized directly in muscle — contribute ~5-10% during prolonged exercise. Carb loading before events: maximizes glycogen stores → delays 'wall.' Reference: Harper's — Exercise Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Cancer cells exhibiting the Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis) also upregulate the pentose phosphate pathway. This dual metabolic shift benefits tumor growth because:
questions.optionA: It produces more ATP than oxidative phosphorylation
questions.optionB: Glycolysis provides rapid ATP and biosynthetic intermediates, while HMP shunt provides NADPH (for reductive biosynthesis and ROS defense) and ribose-5-phosphate (for nucleotide synthesis enabling DNA replication)
questions.optionC: It reduces the need for amino acids
questions.optionD: It prevents apoptosis through ATP generation only
questions.explanation: Warburg effect + HMP shunt — complementary roles in cancer: Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis): cancer cells preferentially use glycolysis even with adequate O2. Not for ATP efficiency but for: (1) Rapid ATP production (glycolysis is faster per unit time than OXPHOS), (2) Biosynthetic intermediates (G6P → ribose; 3-PG → serine → one-carbon metabolism; pyruvate → alanine; DHAP → glycerol-3-P → lipids). HMP shunt upregulation: (1) NADPH: reducing equivalents for fatty acid synthesis, nucleotide synthesis, glutathione reduction (ROS defense — crucial because cancer cells have high ROS), (2) Ribose-5-phosphate: sugar backbone for nucleotide/DNA synthesis (rapidly dividing cells need lots). Key enzymes upregulated: HK2, PKM2, G6PD, TALDO. PKM2 (tumor-specific isoform): less active → diverts glycolytic intermediates to biosynthetic pathways. Reference: Harper's — Cancer Metabolism.

questions.questionText: In uncontrolled DKA (diabetic ketoacidosis), all the following biochemical changes occur EXCEPT:
questions.optionD: Increased gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis
questions.explanation: DKA biochemistry — insulin absence/glucagon excess: (1) Adipose tissue: massive lipolysis (HSL activated) → FFAs released → liver. (2) Liver: FFAs undergo β-oxidation → excess acetyl-CoA → KETOGENESIS (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate, acetone) → ketonemia → metabolic acidosis. Gluconeogenesis activated (PEPCK, G6Pase upregulated) → hyperglycemia. Glycogenolysis: contributes to hyperglycemia. (3) Liver glycolysis is SUPPRESSED (F-2,6-BP decreased because PFK-2 is phosphorylated → kinase OFF, phosphatase ON → less F-2,6-BP → PFK-1 inhibited → glycolysis decreased). This allows gluconeogenesis to predominate. (4) Muscle: proteolysis → amino acids for gluconeogenesis (glucogenic AAs). Clinical: hyperglycemia → osmotic diuresis → dehydration, Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath (acetone). Reference: Harper's — DKA Biochemistry.

questions.questionText: Sepsis causes a unique metabolic state where there is BOTH hyperglycemia AND increased lactate. This paradox occurs because:
questions.optionD: Counter-regulatory hormones and cytokines drive gluconeogenesis (hyperglycemia), while impaired tissue perfusion and mitochondrial dysfunction cause aerobic glycolysis → lactate (even with adequate O2)
questions.explanation: Sepsis metabolism — hyperglycemia + hyperlactatemia: HYPERGLYCEMIA: (1) Stress hormones (cortisol, catecholamines, glucagon, GH) → drive gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis, (2) Cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) → hepatic glucose output increased, (3) Peripheral insulin RESISTANCE (cytokine-induced). HYPERLACTATEMIA: (1) Tissue hypoperfusion → anaerobic glycolysis, (2) Mitochondrial dysfunction (endotoxin/cytokines damage ETC) → aerobic glycolysis even with O2 (similar to Warburg), (3) β2-adrenergic stimulation drives glycolysis, (4) Impaired hepatic lactate clearance (liver overwhelmed or dysfunctional). Lactate >4 mmol/L = severe sepsis marker. Hepatic gluconeogenesis from lactate (Cori cycle) is overwhelmed → lactate accumulates. Initial insulin resistance → later may transition to hypoglycemia (hepatic failure). Reference: Harper's — Sepsis Metabolism.

questions.questionText: Pre-eclampsia is associated with oxidative stress and endothelial dysfunction. The biochemical marker most consistent with this is:
questions.optionB: Elevated serum malondialdehyde (MDA, a lipid peroxidation marker) with decreased glutathione and superoxide dismutase activity
questions.optionD: Low serum LDH
questions.explanation: Oxidative stress markers in pre-eclampsia: Placental ischemia → release of ROS and reactive nitrogen species → oxidative damage to endothelial cells → systemic endothelial dysfunction. Markers of oxidative stress: INCREASED: (1) MDA (malondialdehyde): end product of lipid peroxidation (PUFA → lipid peroxides → MDA). Gold standard marker of oxidative damage measured by TBARS (thiobarbituric acid reactive substances), (2) 8-hydroxydeoxyguanosine (8-OHdG): DNA oxidative damage marker, (3) F2-isoprostanes: in vivo lipid peroxidation. DECREASED: (1) Glutathione (GSH): depleted (consumed as antioxidant), (2) SOD, catalase, GPx: antioxidant enzymes overwhelmed/depleted, (3) Vitamin C from plasma (used as antioxidant). Oxidative stress contributes to hypertension and proteinuria in pre-eclampsia. Reference: Harper's — Oxidative Stress Markers.

questions.questionText: A newborn screening test shows elevated phenylalanine, elevated leucine/isoleucine, and elevated methionine. Which metabolic process is MOST likely defective?
questions.optionB: Individual amino acid catabolism enzymes for each
questions.optionD: A common upstream defect: large neutral amino acid transporter, multiple carboxylase deficiency, or generalized hepatocellular dysfunction causing inability to catabolize multiple amino acids
questions.explanation: Multiple amino acid elevation on newborn screening — differential: When MULTIPLE unrelated amino acids are elevated simultaneously, think COMMON mechanism rather than multiple individual enzyme deficiencies: (1) LIVER DYSFUNCTION (hepatocellular disease): liver catabolizes most amino acids → generalized elevation. Check liver function (ammonia also may be elevated but different from isolated urea cycle defects which show specific OTC/CPS1 patterns). (2) TRANSPORTER defects: large neutral amino acid transporter shared by Phe, Leu, Ile, Met, Trp, etc. (3) Multiple carboxylase deficiency (biotinidase/holocarboxylase synthetase): affects leucine catabolism and other pathways. (4) Generalized metabolic crisis (organic acidemia with secondary inhibition). Isolated Phe elevation → PKU. Isolated BCAA elevation → MSUD. Isolated Met elevation → homocystinuria. Pattern recognition is key. Reference: Harper's — Newborn Screening Integration.

questions.questionText: Acid-base biochemistry: a patient with type B lactic acidosis (no tissue hypoxia) most likely has which underlying condition?
questions.optionB: Thiamine deficiency, metformin toxicity, liver failure, or malignancy — conditions causing mitochondrial dysfunction or impaired lactate clearance WITHOUT hypoperfusion
questions.optionD: Respiratory alkalosis
questions.explanation: Type A vs Type B lactic acidosis: TYPE A (most common): tissue HYPOXIA → anaerobic glycolysis → lactate. Causes: shock (hypovolemic, cardiogenic, septic), severe anemia, CO poisoning, respiratory failure. TYPE B: NO tissue hypoxia. Causes: (B1) Disease-associated: liver failure (impaired lactate clearance), malignancy (Warburg effect), DKA, renal failure. (B2) Drug/toxin: METFORMIN (inhibits complex I → impaired OXPHOS), cyanide, salicylates, propofol, nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (mitochondrial toxicity). (B3) Congenital: THIAMINE deficiency (PDH requires TPP → pyruvate cannot enter TCA → shunted to lactate), pyruvate dehydrogenase deficiency, mitochondrial myopathies, ETC defects. Treatment: treat underlying cause. Metformin-associated: hemodialysis. Thiamine deficiency: thiamine replacement. Reference: Harper's — Lactic Acidosis Types.
questions.conceptTag: Type A vs B lactic acidosis biochemistry

questions.questionText: A 58-year-old strict vegan presents with gait ataxia, loss of vibration sense, and macrocytosis. Labs: Hb 8.9 g/dL, MCV 114 fL, homocysteine high, methylmalonic acid high. Which biochemical defect best explains BOTH hematologic and neurologic findings?
questions.optionA: Impaired conversion of dUMP to dTMP due to isolated folate deficiency
questions.optionB: Failure of methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase reactions due to cobalamin deficiency
questions.optionC: Deficiency of pyridoxal phosphate causing sideroblastic anemia
questions.optionD: Deficiency of riboflavin causing impaired flavoprotein function
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: B12 deficiency impairs methionine synthase (homocysteine rises, methyl-folate trap causes megaloblastosis) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (MMA rises, abnormal myelin lipid handling causes neurologic deficits). Why others are wrong: A explains megaloblastosis but not elevated MMA or posterior column signs; C causes microcytic sideroblastic pattern rather than macrocytosis; D does not produce this marker combination. Key concept: folate and B12 both raise homocysteine, but only B12 raises MMA and causes neurologic disease.

questions.questionText: A 22-year-old woman has recurrent severe abdominal pain, anxiety, tachycardia, and hyponatremia. There is no photosensitivity. During an attack, which test most directly confirms the likely biochemical diagnosis?
questions.optionD: Plasma haptoglobin
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: classic acute intermittent porphyria pattern is neurovisceral attacks without photosensitivity, with increased ALA and PBG during attacks. A is more relevant to cutaneous porphyrias such as PCT. C and D assess hemolysis patterns, not porphyrin pathway flux. Key concept: acute attacks are biochemical overproduction states driven by ALAS1 induction and downstream block.

questions.questionText: A patient with diabetic ketoacidosis has ABG: pH 7.19, HCO3- 10 mEq/L, pCO2 24 mmHg. Using Winter's formula, the expected pCO2 is approximately 23 to 27 mmHg. What is the best interpretation?
questions.optionA: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
questions.optionB: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with superimposed respiratory acidosis
questions.optionD: Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: Winter's formula gives expected pCO2 = 1.5 x HCO3 + 8 +/- 2 = 23 +/- 2, and measured pCO2 24 fits expected compensation. B would require pCO2 above expected range; C and D are inconsistent with low bicarbonate and acidemia. Key concept: biochemical acid-base interpretation should integrate expected compensatory physiology before labeling mixed disorders.

questions.questionText: A 34-year-old man presents with myalgia after strenuous exercise. Labs: AST 220 U/L, ALT 48 U/L, CK-total 6400 U/L, bilirubin normal. Which interpretation is most appropriate?
questions.optionD: Skeletal muscle injury with secondary AST predominance
questions.explanation: Correct answer D: very high CK with AST more than ALT and normal bilirubin strongly supports muscle source (rhabdomyolysis/exertional injury). A usually has ALT rise more prominently with liver context. C requires cholestatic markers and bilirubin pattern. D needs hemolysis markers (reticulocytosis, LDH rise with low haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin). Key concept: enzyme interpretation needs source-context, not isolated numbers.

questions.questionText: A child has seizures precipitated by fasting, developmental delay, and CSF glucose of 32 mg/dL with simultaneous blood glucose 92 mg/dL. Which defect best explains this biochemical pattern?
questions.optionA: GLUT1 transporter deficiency at the blood-brain barrier
questions.optionB: GLUT4 deficiency in muscle and adipose tissue
questions.optionD: GLUT2 deficiency causing Fanconi-Bickel syndrome
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: low CSF glucose despite normal serum glucose indicates impaired glucose transport into brain, classic for GLUT1 deficiency syndrome. B affects insulin-dependent peripheral uptake, not CSF-blood gradient. C affects renal glucose handling. D causes hepatorenal glycogen handling issues with different profile. Clinical correlation: ketogenic diet bypasses cerebral glucose transport defect by providing ketone fuel.

questions.questionText: A 9-year-old boy has behavioral decline, hyperpigmentation, and adrenal insufficiency. MRI shows posterior white matter demyelination. Elevated plasma very-long-chain fatty acids are noted. The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.optionD: Niemann-Pick disease type C
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: X-ALD (ABCD1 transporter defect) causes impaired peroxisomal VLCFA import, leading to VLCFA accumulation, adrenal failure, and cerebral demyelination. A is alpha-oxidation defect with phytanic acid accumulation and retinitis/neuropathy profile. C is arylsulfatase A defect (sulfatide). D is cholesterol trafficking defect with vertical gaze palsy phenotype. Key concept: peroxisomal transporter defects can mimic primary neurologic disease with endocrine clues.

questions.questionText: An agarose lipoprotein electrophoresis report (image description) reads: 'broad beta band with elevated total cholesterol and triglycerides, palmar xanthomas present.' Which molecular defect is most likely?
questions.optionD: ApoE2 homozygosity with remnant clearance defect
questions.explanation: Correct answer D: broad beta disease (Type III dysbetalipoproteinemia) is classically ApoE2/E2 with poor remnant uptake by liver, causing mixed cholesterol and TG elevation plus palmar xanthomas. A causes familial defective ApoB with LDL-predominant pattern. C gives severe chylomicronemia and pancreatitis. D causes very low HDL (Tangier) without broad beta pattern. Key concept: phenotype plus electrophoresis pattern can localize specific apolipoprotein defects.

questions.questionText: All of the following statements about apolipoproteins are true EXCEPT:
questions.optionB: ApoB-48 is required for chylomicron assembly
questions.optionC: ApoE mediates hepatic uptake of remnant particles
questions.optionD: ApoA-I inhibits LCAT to prevent excess HDL maturation
questions.explanation: Correct answer D: ApoA-I activates, not inhibits, LCAT and is central to HDL maturation. A, B, and C are correct and high-yield associations. Key concept summary: Apo proteins are functional ligands/cofactors, not just structural labels.

questions.questionText: A patient has total bilirubin 9.2 mg/dL (direct 0.8), urine bilirubin negative, urine urobilinogen increased, AST and ALT near normal. Which diagnosis is most consistent?
questions.optionD: Acute viral hepatitis
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: predominant unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with absent urine bilirubin and increased urobilinogen indicates prehepatic hemolysis. B would show high direct bilirubin, positive urine bilirubin, and low/absent urobilinogen. C is conjugated hyperbilirubinemia due to canalicular transport defect. D usually has transaminase elevation and mixed bilirubin rise. Key concept: bilirubin fraction plus urine chemistry gives powerful localization.

questions.questionText: A fasting infant develops hypoketotic hypoglycemia with low plasma carnitine and elevated free fatty acids. Which comparison best distinguishes primary carnitine transport defect from MCAD deficiency?
questions.optionA: MCAD causes low acylcarnitines and normal dicarboxylic acids
questions.optionB: Primary carnitine defect shows low total carnitine, while MCAD typically shows elevated medium-chain acylcarnitines and dicarboxylic aciduria
questions.optionC: Both show marked ketosis in fasting
questions.optionD: Both are due to CPT-I inhibition by malonyl-CoA
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: systemic carnitine transporter defect causes markedly low carnitine pool and impaired long-chain FA transport, while MCAD has characteristic medium-chain acylcarnitine accumulation and dicarboxylic acids due to omega-oxidation shunting. A and C are opposite of expected findings (both are hypoketotic states). D is regulatory physiology, not the inherited cause in these disorders. Key concept: enzyme deficiency vs transporter deficiency can be separated by acylcarnitine signature.

questions.questionText: A severely malnourished patient is started on high-carbohydrate feeds and develops confusion, arrhythmia, and muscle weakness within 48 hours. Which biochemical shift is central to this syndrome?
questions.optionA: Extracellular phosphate rise due to lipolysis
questions.optionB: Insulin surge drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells for ATP and glycolytic intermediate synthesis
questions.optionC: Ketone overproduction due to glucagon excess
questions.optionD: Immediate increase in hepatic gluconeogenesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: refeeding syndrome follows abrupt insulin rise after carbohydrate load, causing intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium; phosphate depletion impairs ATP-dependent tissues (myocardium, diaphragm, CNS). A is incorrect direction, C reflects fasting not refeeding, and D is suppressed by insulin. Clinical correlation: prophylactic electrolyte repletion and cautious caloric escalation are essential.

questions.questionText: During progressive fasting, what is the correct sequence of dominant whole-body fuel strategy?
questions.optionB: Glycogenolysis -> gluconeogenesis with lipolysis -> ketogenesis with protein sparing
questions.optionD: Only gluconeogenesis throughout
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: early fasting uses hepatic glycogen; then gluconeogenesis rises with lipolysis support; prolonged fasting shifts heavily to ketogenesis and brain ketone use, reducing proteolysis. A and C misorder physiology; D ignores finite glycogen and adaptation phases. Key concept: metabolic adaptation is staged and hormone-driven, not a single pathway switch.

questions.questionText: Which statement is INCORRECT regarding liver vs muscle fuel metabolism in fasting?
questions.optionA: Liver can export glucose due to glucose-6-phosphatase
questions.optionB: Skeletal muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase and uses glycogen for local ATP
questions.optionC: Brain significantly oxidizes long-chain fatty acids during prolonged fasting
questions.optionD: Liver increases ketogenesis when oxaloacetate is diverted to gluconeogenesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: long-chain fatty acids do not substantially cross the blood-brain barrier; brain adapts to ketone use instead. A, B, and D are correct integrative physiology points. Key concept: organ-specific enzymatic and transport constraints determine fuel partitioning.

questions.questionText: A child with hepatomegaly and fasting hypoglycemia has elevated lactate, uric acid, and triglycerides. His sibling has hepatomegaly and fasting ketotic hypoglycemia but near-normal lactate and uric acid. The best comparison is:
questions.optionA: Both likely have the same glycogen storage disorder
questions.optionB: First is GSD I (glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency), second is GSD III (debranching enzyme deficiency)
questions.optionC: First is Pompe disease, second is McArdle disease
questions.optionD: First is galactosemia, second is hereditary fructose intolerance
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: GSD I causes severe metabolic derangements from trapped G6P (lactic acidosis, hyperuricemia, hyperlipidemia), while GSD III typically has milder biochemical profile with ketotic hypoglycemia and less lactic acidosis. C and D do not match this classic comparative biochemical pattern. Key concept: compare pathway block level to predict full lab phenotype.
questions.conceptTag: GSD I vs GSD III comparison

questions.questionText: All of the following are true about peroxisomal beta-oxidation EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: It preferentially shortens very-long-chain fatty acids
questions.optionB: Initial electron transfer generates hydrogen peroxide handled by catalase
questions.optionC: It is a major ATP-generating pathway equivalent to mitochondrial beta-oxidation
questions.optionD: Shortened products are transferred to mitochondria for further oxidation
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: peroxisomal beta-oxidation is primarily chain-shortening and detoxification, not a major ATP-coupled energy source like mitochondrial oxidation. A, B, and D are correct. Key concept: compartmentalization explains why similar chemistry has different physiologic roles.

questions.questionText: In urea cycle disorders, which integrated pathway connection explains why argininosuccinate lyase deficiency can alter TCA intermediate balance?
questions.optionA: Argininosuccinate cleavage produces fumarate that links urea cycle to TCA and gluconeogenesis
questions.optionD: Ornithine is converted to pyruvate in muscle
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: argininosuccinate lyase forms arginine plus fumarate, and fumarate enters the TCA-aspartate shunt, linking nitrogen disposal with carbon metabolism. B, C, and D are incorrect biochemical statements. Key concept: pathway integration questions often test bridge metabolites rather than isolated enzyme names.

questions.questionText: A 46-year-old with acute chest pain has CK-MB rise at 6 hours, while LDH remains near normal initially. At 72 hours, CK-MB normalizes but LDH1 exceeds LDH2 (flip pattern). What is the best biochemical interpretation?
questions.optionA: Pattern indicates isolated skeletal muscle disease
questions.optionB: No myocardial injury because markers are discordant
questions.optionC: Temporal enzyme kinetics strongly support myocardial infarction
questions.optionD: Pattern is diagnostic of hemolytic anemia only
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: CK-MB rises early and falls within 48 to 72 h; LDH1 flip appears later, so this sequential pattern is classic for MI kinetics. A ignores timing physiology. C usually has CK elevation without typical LDH1/LDH2 flip context. D can raise LDH but not this coordinated cardiac-marker timeline. Key concept: time-resolved lab interpretation outperforms single-point marker reading.

questions.questionText: Most appropriate next biochemical management step in suspected acute hyperammonemic encephalopathy (while etiology workup is pending) is:
questions.optionB: Immediate nitrogen-scavenging strategy and ammonia-lowering therapy (for example lactulose in hepatic causes; benzoate/phenylbutyrate in urea-cycle context)
questions.optionD: Administer folate first
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: hyperammonemia is neurotoxic emergency; early biochemical ammonia-lowering interventions prevent cerebral edema and herniation. A worsens nitrogen load initially. C delays life-saving therapy. D is unrelated. Clinical correlation: definitive etiology can be established in parallel, but ammonia control must be immediate.

questions.questionText: Assertion-Reason: Assertion (A): Folate therapy alone may improve anemia in unrecognized B12 deficiency. Reason (R): Folate repletion restores nucleotide synthesis sufficiently to correct megaloblastosis, but does not correct methylmalonyl-CoA related neurologic injury.
questions.optionA: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
questions.optionB: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
questions.optionC: A is true, R is false
questions.optionD: A is false, R is true
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: this is the classic masking phenomenon. Folate can rescue DNA synthesis and blood counts, but neurologic progression continues in B12 deficiency because methylmalonyl-CoA mutase dysfunction is not corrected. Why others are wrong: B ignores direct mechanistic link; C and D contradict established folate-B12 interaction. Key concept: always evaluate B12 before isolated folate correction in megaloblastic states.

questions.questionText: Which sequence best describes biochemical adaptation from overnight fast to prolonged starvation in terms of hormonal control and pathway dominance?
questions.optionB: Rising glucagon/low insulin -> glycogenolysis -> gluconeogenesis with lipolysis -> ketogenesis with reduced muscle proteolysis
questions.optionC: Constant cortisol only -> glycolysis -> cholesterol synthesis
questions.optionD: High catecholamine only -> PPP activation -> purine synthesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: this integrated sequence captures endocrine and pathway transitions across fasting duration. A is fed-state pattern. C and D omit dominant fasting biology and misuse pathway priorities. Key concept: exam stems often hide timeline clues (overnight vs prolonged) that change the correct pathway hierarchy.

questions.questionText: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with confusion and severe dehydration. Labs: glucose 860 mg/dL, serum osmolality 336 mOsm/kg, pH 7.36, HCO3- 21 mEq/L, ketones trace. Which mechanism best explains minimal ketoacidosis despite extreme hyperglycemia?
questions.optionA: Marked insulin excess drives complete peripheral glucose uptake
questions.optionB: Absolute glucagon deficiency prevents hepatic glucose output
questions.optionC: Residual insulin suppresses adipose lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis but is insufficient for effective glucose disposal
questions.optionD: Renal bicarbonate regeneration fully blocks acid production
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: HHS typically has relative insulin deficiency, not absolute absence. This suppresses major ketogenesis yet cannot prevent profound hyperglycemia and osmotic diuresis. A and B are incorrect because glucose remains very high and hepatic output is not shut down. D is incorrect because the key distinction is reduced ketone generation, not complete buffering. Key principle: DKA is insulin-deficient ketotic acidosis; HHS is hyperosmolar decompensation with little ketosis.

questions.questionText: A patient with DKA has initial serum K+ 5.4 mEq/L. Two hours after insulin and fluids, K+ falls to 3.3 mEq/L. Which integrated explanation is most accurate?
questions.optionA: Total body potassium was depleted by osmotic diuresis, and insulin shifted extracellular potassium into cells
questions.optionB: Insulin causes renal potassium secretion independent of aldosterone within minutes
questions.optionC: Ketoacids directly chelate plasma potassium causing low measured value
questions.optionD: Improved oxygenation converts potassium into intracellular phosphate salts
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: DKA often presents with normal/high serum K despite severe total body deficit. Insulin therapy and correction of acidosis move K intracellularly, unmasking depletion from prior urinary losses. B overstates rapid renal-only effect; C and D are physiologically incorrect. Key principle: monitor and replace K aggressively during DKA management.
questions.conceptTag: DKA potassium shift total body deficit

questions.questionText: A patient with tinnitus and tachypnea after tablet overdose has ABG: pH 7.46, pCO2 24 mmHg, HCO3- 17 mEq/L, anion gap elevated. Best acid-base interpretation is:
questions.optionA: Pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis
questions.optionD: Mixed respiratory alkalosis with high anion gap metabolic acidosis
questions.explanation: Correct answer D: salicylate toxicity classically produces early respiratory alkalosis plus concurrent/late high anion gap metabolic acidosis. A or B alone fails to explain both low pCO2 and low HCO3 with near-normal/slightly alkalemic pH. C is wrong because anion gap is high. Key principle: near-normal pH can hide dangerous mixed disorders.

questions.questionText: A patient with profuse diarrhea has pH 7.30, HCO3- 14 mEq/L, chloride 114 mEq/L, anion gap normal. Which mechanism best explains the acidosis?
questions.optionA: Loss of bicarbonate-rich intestinal fluid with reciprocal hyperchloremia
questions.optionB: Accumulation of beta-hydroxybutyrate from insulin deficiency
questions.optionD: Retention of sulfate and phosphate in advanced kidney failure
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: bicarbonate-rich intestinal fluid loss causes hyperchloremic non-anion gap metabolic acidosis. B is DKA-related high anion gap acidosis. C causes lactic high anion gap acidosis. D is uremic high anion gap acidosis. Key principle: normal anion gap acidosis often reflects bicarbonate loss or reduced renal acid secretion.

questions.questionText: In severe insulin deficiency, oxaloacetate depletion favors ketogenesis primarily because:
questions.optionB: Oxaloacetate is diverted to gluconeogenesis, limiting TCA condensation with acetyl-CoA and channeling acetyl-CoA toward ketone synthesis
questions.optionC: Pyruvate kinase overactivation consumes mitochondrial NADH
questions.optionD: Citrate exported from mitochondria drives ketone utilization in liver
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: when OAA is pulled toward gluconeogenesis, TCA flux for acetyl-CoA oxidation decreases and hepatic ketogenesis rises. A, C, and D do not explain the core carbon-partition mechanism. Key principle: ketogenesis in DKA is not only excess acetyl-CoA supply but also relative TCA bottleneck.

questions.questionText: ABG shows pH 7.10, HCO3- 12 mEq/L, measured pCO2 38 mmHg. Winter's expected pCO2 is approximately 26 +/- 2. Interpretation?
questions.optionB: Mixed metabolic acidosis with respiratory alkalosis
questions.optionD: Metabolic acidosis with superimposed respiratory acidosis
questions.explanation: Correct answer D: measured pCO2 is much higher than expected, indicating inadequate ventilatory compensation and additional respiratory acidosis. A is wrong because compensation is not appropriate. B would require lower-than-expected pCO2. C ignores clearly low bicarbonate. Key principle: Winter's formula is essential for detecting mixed disorders in ICU-style stems.

questions.questionText: A teenager with newly diagnosed untreated type 1 diabetes has polyphagia, weight loss, and muscle wasting. Which metabolic profile is most consistent?
questions.optionA: Increased glycogen synthesis with positive nitrogen balance
questions.optionB: Suppressed gluconeogenesis with low hepatic glucose output
questions.optionC: Increased proteolysis and lipolysis with negative nitrogen balance and hepatic ketogenesis
questions.optionD: Reduced hormone-sensitive lipase activity in adipose tissue
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: absolute insulin deficiency creates catabolic dominance, with proteolysis, lipolysis, gluconeogenesis, and ketogenesis causing weight loss despite polyphagia. A, C, and D reflect insulinized/fed-state physiology. Key principle: untreated type 1 diabetes is a starvation-in-the-midst-of-abundance state.

questions.questionText: During prolonged fasting, renal gluconeogenesis rises. Which substrate-pathway pairing is most accurate?
questions.optionA: Leucine converted to glucose in proximal tubule
questions.optionB: Glutamine metabolism in renal cortex contributes carbon for gluconeogenesis and ammoniagenesis for acid buffering
questions.optionC: Palmitate converted to glucose via acetyl-CoA reversal
questions.optionD: Ketone bodies converted to glucose in distal nephron
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: kidney contributes significantly in prolonged fasting using glutamine-derived intermediates, while generating ammonia for acid excretion. A is wrong because leucine is purely ketogenic. C is wrong because even-chain fatty acids do not yield net glucose. D is wrong because ketones are oxidized, not converted to glucose. Key principle: renal gluconeogenesis is a major late-fasting adaptation.

questions.questionText: Which laboratory pattern most strongly supports CPS-I deficiency over OTC deficiency and hereditary orotic aciduria?
questions.optionA: High ammonia, high orotic acid, low citrulline
questions.optionB: Normal ammonia, high orotic acid, megaloblastic anemia
questions.optionC: High ammonia, low orotic acid, low citrulline
questions.optionD: High ammonia, high citrulline, low argininosuccinate
questions.explanation: Correct answer C is the defining CPS-I pattern: severe hyperammonemia with low citrulline and no excess orotate. A suggests OTC deficiency. B suggests UMP synthase defect (hereditary orotic aciduria) with no hyperammonemia. D fits citrullinemia (ASS deficiency). Key principle: combine ammonia plus orotate plus citrulline to localize urea-cycle blocks.
questions.conceptTag: CPS1 vs OTC vs UMP synthase pattern

questions.questionText: Regarding DKA, HHS, and starvation ketosis, which statement is most accurate?
questions.optionA: DKA usually has marked ketonemia and high anion gap acidosis; HHS has profound hyperosmolarity with minimal ketosis; starvation ketosis typically has lower glucose and milder acidosis
questions.optionB: HHS shows more severe ketonemia than DKA because glucose is higher
questions.optionC: Starvation ketosis requires absolute insulin deficiency and severe hyperglycemia
questions.optionD: DKA and starvation ketosis are biochemically indistinguishable
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: this is the core exam comparison. B is false because residual insulin in HHS limits ketogenesis. C is false because starvation ketosis occurs with low-normal glucose and relative insulin preservation. D is false because glucose level, dehydration severity, and ketone-acid burden differ substantially. Key clue: hyperosmolarity dominates HHS; acidosis dominates DKA.

questions.questionText: A neonate with hyperammonemia has markedly elevated citrulline, low arginine, and no significant orotic acid increase. Most likely defect?
questions.optionD: Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase I deficiency
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: ASS deficiency causes citrulline accumulation with hyperammonemia. A usually has high orotate and low citrulline. C usually has low citrulline without orotate rise. D typically presents later with spasticity and elevated arginine rather than severe neonatal hyperammonemic crisis. Key principle: citrulline level is a high-yield discriminator.

questions.questionText: In hyperammonemic encephalopathy, the immediate cause of cerebral edema is best explained by:
questions.optionA: Neuronal ATP depletion from citrate accumulation
questions.optionB: Excess urea entry into CSF raising osmolarity
questions.optionD: Astrocytic glutamine accumulation causing osmotic swelling and impaired neurotransmitter handling
questions.explanation: Correct answer D: astrocytes detoxify ammonia via glutamine synthetase; glutamine accumulation increases osmotic load and contributes to edema and encephalopathy. A and C are not the principal mechanism. B is not the key pathophysiology in acute hyperammonemia. Key principle: ammonia neurotoxicity is astrocyte-centered, not merely serum-level toxicity.

questions.questionText: Which option correctly identifies the MOST specific distinguishing clue for MSUD compared with PKU, alkaptonuria, and homocystinuria?
questions.optionA: Intermittent maple-syrup odor with neonatal ketoacidosis and markedly elevated branched-chain amino acids including alloisoleucine
questions.optionB: Musty odor with hypopigmentation and isolated phenylalanine elevation
questions.optionC: Dark urine on standing with ochronosis and arthropathy
questions.optionD: Lens subluxation with high homocysteine and marfanoid habitus
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: alloisoleucine plus branched-chain ketoacid dehydrogenase block is highly characteristic of MSUD. B is PKU, C is alkaptonuria, and D is homocystinuria. Key principle: exam stems often offer four classic inborn-error signatures; pattern recognition is faster than pathway memorization.

questions.questionText: Why can odd-chain fatty acids contribute to net glucose production whereas even-chain fatty acids cannot?
questions.optionA: Odd-chain fatty acids directly produce pyruvate in peroxisomes
questions.optionB: Odd-chain fatty acids bypass beta-oxidation and enter glycolysis
questions.optionC: Odd-chain beta-oxidation yields propionyl-CoA, which is converted to succinyl-CoA and can feed gluconeogenesis
questions.optionD: Even-chain fatty acids are unable to form acetyl-CoA in liver
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: propionyl-CoA -> methylmalonyl-CoA -> succinyl-CoA provides glucogenic carbon. A and B are incorrect pathway claims. D is false because even-chain fatty acids do form acetyl-CoA, but acetyl-CoA has no net glucose yield in humans. Key principle: odd-chain exception is a classic trap in fat-to-glucose questions.

questions.questionText: Match the toxin to its principal biochemical target: cyanide, arsenite, fluoroacetate. Which pairing is correct?
questions.optionD: Cyanide-cytochrome c oxidase; arsenite-lipoic acid-dependent dehydrogenases; fluoroacetate (as fluorocitrate)-aconitase
questions.explanation: Correct answer D is the true mapping: cyanide blocks Complex IV, arsenite inhibits lipoic-acid-dependent enzymes such as PDH and alpha-KGDH, and fluorocitrate inhibits aconitase. A-C deliberately swap targets. Key principle: toxicology stems test exact enzyme-level localization, not only pathway names.

questions.questionText: A patient with CKD on metformin presents with malaise and deep breathing. Labs: lactate 7.1 mmol/L, pH 7.24, glucose 148 mg/dL, ketones negative. Most likely mechanism?
questions.optionC: Metformin-associated mitochondrial Complex I inhibition with reduced hepatic lactate utilization
questions.optionD: Absolute insulin deficiency causing DKA
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: high lactate acidosis with modest glucose and absent ketones favors metformin-associated lactic acidosis, especially with renal impairment. A and D do not fit ketone profile. B usually has ketones and often euglycemic ketoacidosis pattern. Key principle: metformin and sulfonylureas differ fundamentally in mechanism and biochemical adverse-event profile.

questions.questionText: A 7-month-old develops vomiting, sweating, and lethargy soon after fruit puree introduction. Exam shows hepatomegaly; cataracts are absent. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.optionD: Glycogen storage disease type II
questions.explanation: Correct answer B: HFI presents after fructose/sucrose introduction with hypoglycemia, vomiting, and liver dysfunction. A usually presents in early milk feeding and often includes cataracts. C is benign. D is a lysosomal glycogenosis with cardiomyopathy pattern. Key principle: age at symptom onset relative to diet is a decisive clue.

questions.questionText: Which biochemical pattern best favors methylmalonic acidemia over isolated propionic acidemia?
questions.optionA: Marked methylmalonic acid elevation with or without high homocysteine, especially in cobalamin-related defects
questions.optionB: Isolated hyperammonemia with normal organic acids
questions.optionC: Only elevated phenylalanine with normal acylcarnitine profile
questions.optionD: Persistent ketonuria with normal anion gap and no acylcarnitine changes
questions.explanation: Correct answer A: methylmalonyl-CoA mutase or B12 pathway defects produce MMA accumulation; propionic acidemia elevates propionyl markers without prominent MMA rise. B, C, and D do not describe this organic acidemia differential. Key principle: urine organic acids plus acylcarnitine signatures are high-yield newborn-screen tools.

questions.questionText: A febrile patient in septic shock has hyperglycemia despite high endogenous insulin levels and rising lactate. Which signaling change best explains this paradox?
questions.optionA: Tyrosine hyperphosphorylation of IRS-1 enhancing PI3K-AKT signaling
questions.optionB: Complete GLUT4 gene deletion in skeletal muscle
questions.optionC: Cytokine-driven serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 (via JNK/IKK pathways) causing insulin resistance while stress hormones maintain hepatic glucose output
questions.optionD: Constitutive mTOR inhibition increasing insulin receptor density
questions.explanation: Correct answer C: inflammatory signaling induces IRS-1 serine phosphorylation, weakening insulin action in peripheral tissues; concurrent stress hormones/catecholamines drive hepatic glucose production, while perfusion/mitochondrial defects increase lactate. A is opposite effect, B is too absolute and non-physiologic for sepsis, D does not explain the acute metabolic pattern. Key principle: sepsis combines insulin resistance and hyperlactatemia through intertwined endocrine and inflammatory pathways.
